The project should consider different 
gender 
relationships and roles in the project area while 
preparing for consultations.
Women and men typically fulfil different roles and 
responsibilities, depending on the context and the 
country.
Virtually no role is always exclusively performed by 
just women or men.
Because women often are assigned lower social 
status relative to men, women tend to have lower 
confidence, less influence and less involvement in

including them in the plantation establishment 
activities, such as planting.
But the project commissioned a social impact 
assessment/5/ where all the gender-related risks 
were identified following the categorization presented 
in the World Bank’s Papua New Guinea - Country 
gender assessment for the period 2011-2012 (CGA).
The validation team has reviewed this and accepted 
the same.
As gender inequality was identified in the region, the 
stakeholder 
consultation 
was 
also 
developed

Principle 9.1 - Landscape Modification and Soil   The Gold Standard Certification requires that   3.9.1  The  Project  shall  identify  the  functions  and  services provided by the landscape and demonstrate  no  net  degradation  in  existing  landscape  function  and services.   .9.2  To  ensure  healthy  soils  the  following  aspects  shall  be  identified,  and  appropriate  measures  shall  be put in place to protect them:   a) Soil types, AND   b) Biota, AND   c)  Erosion   .9.3  Measures  shall  be  incorporated  to  minimise  soil  degradation  (e.g.,  through  crop  rotation,  composting,  no  use  of  heavy  machinery,  use  of  N- fixing  plants,  reduced  tillage,  no  use  of  ecologically  harmful substances).   .9.4 Projects that involve the production, harvesting,  and/or  management  of  living  natural  resources  by  small-scale  landholders  and/or  local  communities  shall  adopt  the  appropriate  and  culturally  sensitive  sustainable resource management practices.   oes the Project involve the use of land and soil for  production of crops or other products?   Assessment  question  as  per  GS  safeguarding  principles and Requirements v1.2) | Validation opinion: T The  verification  team  has  confirmed  that  project  activities  would  not  impact  the  soil  types much.  The  establishment of plantation also reduces soil erosion.  The  slope  topography  of  the  project  area  ensured  that there is not soil compaction due to movement of  heavy machinery and no soil pressure. The different  soil  types  have  been  mapped  out  and  prevention  of  soil erosion. The biota planted also acts as a barrier  against  soil  erosion.  Though  soil  erosion  is  not  completely  prevented,  some  restitution  efforts  have  been done for restoration. In general, the established  plantation follows the land contours by designing the  rip lines accordingly.   Key  management  measures  to  minimise  impacts  to  soils  from  erosion,  compaction  and/or  mixing  and  nutrient loss are described below.  Vegetation Clearing and Construction  1. Vegetation clearing will only be undertaken to the  extent  required  for  the  designated  land  use;  boundaries  of  areas  to  be  cleared  will  be  physically  demarcated prior to commencement of clearing.  2. At the completion of ground disturbance activities,  where  feasible,  all  disturbed  areas  will  be  rehabilitated  to  minimise  ongoing  soil  erosion  and  promote the natural revegetation of these areas.  3.  Where  practicable,  road  construction  and  major  earthworks will occur during the dry season.  4.  Where  possible,  areas  to  be  cleared  will  exclude  locations  of  high  erosion  potential  (e.g.,  steep  slopes).  5.Project facilities will be located in already disturbed  areas as far as practicable.  6.All  removed  topsoil  and  vegetation  will  be  stockpiled  for  later  use  during  rehabilitation  or  recycled.  7.Erosion  and  sediment  control  measures  together  with  locations  and  types  of  control  devices  (e.g.,  sediment  pond,  silt  fence,  diversion  drain)  will  be  installed, where required.  8.Groundcover vegetation will be retained if possible.  9.The length of time that cleared/disturbed areas are  exposed  will  be  minimised  to  the  greatest  extent  practicable.  10.Topsoil  will  be  carefully  removed  and  stockpiled  when an area is being cleared for subsequent use in  rehabilitation where feasible.  11.Revegetation of stockpiles will be promoted to  protect soil from erosion, discourage weeds  and maintain soil microbe populations.  12.Vegetation  clearing  will  adhere  to  the  PNG  Logging Code of Practice (PNGFA/DEC, 1996).  he  validation  team  has  reviewed  the  environment  management  plan  prepared  by  ERIAS  group  in  March  2017  and  accepted  that  mitigation  measures  to safeguard the environment aspects as robust.  This safeguarding principle is correctly addressed by  the PP in the PDD. Hence accepted by the validation  team.

3) SDG 15: Life on Land – The project continues to contribute to the sustainable 
use of terrestrial ecosystems and reverses land degradation.
The establishment 
of forest plantations in pasture areas will gradually improve soil condition, 
diminishing land degradation of the grasslands in the Markham Valley.

Principle 9.1 - Landscape Modification and Soil   The Gold Standard Certification requires that   3.9.1  The  Project  shall  identify  the  functions  and  services provided by the landscape and demonstrate  no  net  degradation  in  existing  landscape  function  and services.   .9.2  To  ensure  healthy  soils  the  following  aspects  shall  be  identified,  and  appropriate  measures  shall  be put in place to protect them:   a) Soil types, AND   b) Biota, AND   c)  Erosion   .9.3  Measures  shall  be  incorporated  to  minimise  soil  degradation  (e.g.,  through  crop  rotation,  composting,  no  use  of  heavy  machinery,  use  of  N- fixing  plants,  reduced  tillage,  no  use  of  ecologically  harmful substances).   .9.4 Projects that involve the production, harvesting,  and/or  management  of  living  natural  resources  by  small-scale  landholders  and/or  local  communities  shall  adopt  the  appropriate  and  culturally  sensitive  sustainable resource management practices.   oes the Project involve the use of land and soil for  production of crops or other products?   Assessment  question  as  per  GS  safeguarding  principles and Requirements v1.2) | Validation opinion: T The  verification  team  has  confirmed  that  project  activities  would  not  impact  the  soil  types much.  The  establishment of plantation also reduces soil erosion.  The  slope  topography  of  the  project  area  ensured  that there is not soil compaction due to movement of  heavy machinery and no soil pressure. The different  soil  types  have  been  mapped  out  and  prevention  of  soil erosion. The biota planted also acts as a barrier  against  soil  erosion.  Though  soil  erosion  is  not  completely  prevented,  some  restitution  efforts  have  been done for restoration. In general, the established  plantation follows the land contours by designing the  rip lines accordingly.   Key  management  measures  to  minimise  impacts  to  soils  from  erosion,  compaction  and/or  mixing  and  nutrient loss are described below.  Vegetation Clearing and Construction  1. Vegetation clearing will only be undertaken to the  extent  required  for  the  designated  land  use;  boundaries  of  areas  to  be  cleared  will  be  physically  demarcated prior to commencement of clearing.  2. At the completion of ground disturbance activities,  where  feasible,  all  disturbed  areas  will  be  rehabilitated  to  minimise  ongoing  soil  erosion  and  promote the natural revegetation of these areas.  3.  Where  practicable,  road  construction  and  major  earthworks will occur during the dry season.  4.  Where  possible,  areas  to  be  cleared  will  exclude  locations  of  high  erosion  potential  (e.g.,  steep  slopes).  5.Project facilities will be located in already disturbed  areas as far as practicable.  6.All  removed  topsoil  and  vegetation  will  be  stockpiled  for  later  use  during  rehabilitation  or  recycled.  7.Erosion  and  sediment  control  measures  together  with  locations  and  types  of  control  devices  (e.g.,  sediment  pond,  silt  fence,  diversion  drain)  will  be  installed, where required.  8.Groundcover vegetation will be retained if possible.  9.The length of time that cleared/disturbed areas are  exposed  will  be  minimised  to  the  greatest  extent  practicable.  10.Topsoil  will  be  carefully  removed  and  stockpiled  when an area is being cleared for subsequent use in  rehabilitation where feasible.  11.Revegetation of stockpiles will be promoted to  protect soil from erosion, discourage weeds  and maintain soil microbe populations.  12.Vegetation  clearing  will  adhere  to  the  PNG  Logging Code of Practice (PNGFA/DEC, 1996).  he  validation  team  has  reviewed  the  environment  management  plan  prepared  by  ERIAS  group  in  March  2017  and  accepted  that  mitigation  measures  to safeguard the environment aspects as robust.  This safeguarding principle is correctly addressed by  the PP in the PDD. Hence accepted by the validation  team.

Since  these  activities  are  developed  by  the  community  on  their  own  lands,  and  by  themselves,  this  alternative  does  not  face  the  barriers  related  to  social  conditions  and  land  tenure.  Moreover,  resulting  crop  production  is  sold  in  local markets, which does not require transportation to main cities  nor the transport of supplies, since house gardens are established  with  little  to  no  agricultural  supplies.  The  community  also  overcomes  ecological  barriers  through  using  slash-and-burn  agricultural  techniques  and  rotating  their  crops.    The  validation  team has accepted that this alternative does not face any barrier  and Use Scenario 3: Oil palm plantations  aining  access  to  customary-owned  land  and  barriers  related  to  transport infrastructure are the principal barriers at a local level for  its implementation. Hence this alternative is excluded.

Gold  Standard  Afforestation/Reforestation  (A/R)  GHG  Emissions Reduction & Sequestration Methodology v1.0: 2.5 Leakage  1.  Leakage  are  emissions  that  occur  due  to  a  shift  of  activities  from  the  inside  of  a  project  area  to  the  outside of a project area.  These  shifts  of  activities  can  cause  four  different  categories of Leakage by:  (a) collection of wood (for firewood, charcoal, etc.)  (b) timber harvesting  (c) agriculture (crop cultivation, shrimp cultivation, etc.)  (d) livestock.  These four categories are used in the formulas below.  Note  that  only  the  ‘tree  biomass’  affected  by  these  activity shifts shall be considered.  2.  Leakage  shall  be  determined  on  a  Modelling  Unit  (MU) level using the following formula:  Leakage  =  Leakage  Project  area  /  Eligible  planting  area  Leakage is deducted in the first year (t=1).  2.  With  the  application  of  formulas  below  all  potential  Leakage  caused  by  a  project  within  its  crediting  period is accounted for in year 1.   Thus the parameter is not subject to monitoring.  4. Formula for category (a), (b) and (c)  Leakage Project area = Area * % of activity-shift [%] *  CO2-stock   Area = Land within the project area where the activity  is  taking  place  %  of  activity-shift  =  Percentage  of  the  activity that  • will be displaced during the crediting period, AND  •  will  have  impact  on  the  ‘tree  biomass’  outside  the  project area  The factor is determined by:  • credible estimations, OR  • a representative survey  CO2-stock  =  Average  stock  of  ‘tree  biomass’  on  the  area where the activity will be displaced to  If it is not known where the activity will be displaced to,  the CO2-stock = the average stock of ‘tree biomass’ of  a natural forest in the projects host-country  Formula for category (d)  Leakage  Project  area  =  Displaced  heads  *  Grazing  capacity * CO2-stock  Displaced  heads  =  Amount  of  heads  that  will  be  displaced  during  the  crediting  period,  AND  will  have  impact on the ‘tree biomass’ outside the project area  The factor is determined by:  • credible estimations, OR  • a representative survey  Grazing capacity = Grazing capacity of the area where  the livestock will be displaced to  CO2-stock  =  Average  stock  of  ‘tree  biomass’  on  the  area where the activity will be displaced to  If it is not known where the activity will be displaced to, | Validation opinion: There  is  no  leakage  for  this  project  as  there  is  no  collection of wood, timber harvesting, agriculture or  livestock.

Hence, preserving and increasing adaptive  capacity  for  project  participants  must  be  an  integral  element of every project. Specifically:   a)  The  Project  Developer  shall  identify  the  current  and  predicted  variability  in  climate/weather  for  the  project  region.   b)  Based  on  the  current  and  predicted  variability  in  climate/weather,  the  Project  Developer  shall  analyse  the  possible effects on the project within the crediting period.   c)  The  Project  Developer  shall  implement  adaptation  activities  appropriate  to  the  context  and  need  of  the  respective project. Adaptation activities may include:   .  Practices  that  increase  the  resilience  of  farming  systems, OR   i. Measures to improve the efficiency of water use, OR   ii. Crops (crop breeds) with improved characteristics, OR   v. Crop rotation schemes, OR   .  Sharing  of  existing  farmers’  knowledge  as  well  as  knowledge on new agriculture practices, OR   i.  Diversification  of  livelihoods,  e.g.,  through  increased  agricultural  productivity,  increased  variety  of  cultivated  crops, identification of other income streams, OR   ii. This applies to the release of pollutants to  air,  water,  and  land  due  to  routine,  non-routine  and  accidental circumstances.   .9.9  The  Project  Developer  shall  ensure  that  pollution  prevention  and  control  technologies  and  practices  consistent  with  national  regulation  or  international  good  practice  are  applied  during  the  Project life cycle.   .9.10  All potential  pollution sources  that may result  from  the  Project  that  cause  the  degradation  of  the  quality  of  soil,  air,  surface  and  groundwater  within  the  Project’s  area  of  influence  shall  be  identified.  Appropriate  mitigation  measures  and  monitoring  shall  be  implemented  to  ensure  the  protection  of  resources.  The  project  can  use  historical  records,  ongoing  monitoring  and  reporting  through  data  logging  of  physical  measurements,  online  sources,  government  data.  The  recommended  methods  include quantitative documentation of all sources and  volumes  of  water  abstractions,  use  of  weirs  and  gauges,  flow  meters,  pump  energy  consumption, | Validation opinion: The  validation  team  has  reviewed  the  environment  impact assessment report/5/ and PP discussed about  mitigation measures that will be implemented during  both  the  construction  and  operations  phases  of  the  Project include :  1.Use  water  to  suppress  dust  emissions  during  periods  of  extended  dry  weather  and  when  dust  nuisance  has  the  potential  to  occur  as  a  result  of  construction activities.  2.Locate  stockpiles  away  from  areas  prone  to  elevated erosion or sensitive receptor locations.  3.Maintain  all  vehicles  and  machinery  used  for  the  construction  in  accordance  with  the  manufacturer's  specifications to limit exhaust emissions.  4.  Limit  truck  queuing,  unnecessary  idling  of  trucks  and  unnecessary  trips  through  logistical  planning  of  materials delivery and work practices.  5.Implement  speed  limits  via  posted  speed  limit  signs on Project unsealed roads (when required).  6.Where  loads  of  materials  likely  to  generate  dust  (e.g., fly ash or bottom ash) are being transported to  or  from  the  power  plant,  these  will  be  covered  if  practicable.  7.Ensure  that  vehicles  keep  to  marked  trafficable  areas  which  are  maintained  in  a  compacted  condition (when required) to enhance safety and limit

Principle 9.1 - Landscape Modification and Soil   The Gold Standard Certification requires that   3.9.1  The  Project  shall  identify  the  functions  and  services provided by the landscape and demonstrate  no  net  degradation  in  existing  landscape  function  and services.   .9.2  To  ensure  healthy  soils  the  following  aspects  shall  be  identified,  and  appropriate  measures  shall  be put in place to protect them:   a) Soil types, AND   b) Biota, AND   c)  Erosion   .9.3  Measures  shall  be  incorporated  to  minimise  soil  degradation  (e.g.,  through  crop  rotation,  composting,  no  use  of  heavy  machinery,  use  of  N- fixing  plants,  reduced  tillage,  no  use  of  ecologically  harmful substances).   .9.4 Projects that involve the production, harvesting,  and/or  management  of  living  natural  resources  by  small-scale  landholders  and/or  local  communities  shall  adopt  the  appropriate  and  culturally  sensitive  sustainable resource management practices.   oes the Project involve the use of land and soil for  production of crops or other products?   Assessment  question  as  per  GS  safeguarding  principles and Requirements v1.2) | Validation opinion: T The  verification  team  has  confirmed  that  project  activities  would  not  impact  the  soil  types much.  The  establishment of plantation also reduces soil erosion.  The  slope  topography  of  the  project  area  ensured  that there is not soil compaction due to movement of  heavy machinery and no soil pressure. The different  soil  types  have  been  mapped  out  and  prevention  of  soil erosion. The biota planted also acts as a barrier  against  soil  erosion.  Though  soil  erosion  is  not  completely  prevented,  some  restitution  efforts  have  been done for restoration. In general, the established  plantation follows the land contours by designing the  rip lines accordingly.   Key  management  measures  to  minimise  impacts  to  soils  from  erosion,  compaction  and/or  mixing  and  nutrient loss are described below.  Vegetation Clearing and Construction  1. Vegetation clearing will only be undertaken to the  extent  required  for  the  designated  land  use;  boundaries  of  areas  to  be  cleared  will  be  physically  demarcated prior to commencement of clearing.  2. At the completion of ground disturbance activities,  where  feasible,  all  disturbed  areas  will  be  rehabilitated  to  minimise  ongoing  soil  erosion  and  promote the natural revegetation of these areas.  3.  Where  practicable,  road  construction  and  major  earthworks will occur during the dry season.  4.  Where  possible,  areas  to  be  cleared  will  exclude  locations  of  high  erosion  potential  (e.g.,  steep  slopes).  5.Project facilities will be located in already disturbed  areas as far as practicable.  6.All  removed  topsoil  and  vegetation  will  be  stockpiled  for  later  use  during  rehabilitation  or  recycled.  7.Erosion  and  sediment  control  measures  together  with  locations  and  types  of  control  devices  (e.g.,  sediment  pond,  silt  fence,  diversion  drain)  will  be  installed, where required.  8.Groundcover vegetation will be retained if possible.  9.The length of time that cleared/disturbed areas are  exposed  will  be  minimised  to  the  greatest  extent  practicable.  10.Topsoil  will  be  carefully  removed  and  stockpiled  when an area is being cleared for subsequent use in  rehabilitation where feasible.  11.Revegetation of stockpiles will be promoted to  protect soil from erosion, discourage weeds  and maintain soil microbe populations.  12.Vegetation  clearing  will  adhere  to  the  PNG  Logging Code of Practice (PNGFA/DEC, 1996).  he  validation  team  has  reviewed  the  environment  management  plan  prepared  by  ERIAS  group  in  March  2017  and  accepted  that  mitigation  measures  to safeguard the environment aspects as robust. GS Land Use and Forests Activity Requirements v1.2:  | Validation opinion: (planters) in the project boundary. The  objective  of  the  project  is  production  of  firewood  which  would be used for 30 MW biomass power plant.  The  project  also  increases  the  forest  plantation  area  and  helps  in  technology  transfer  to  other  community  plantations.  There  are  2  community  people  located  in  the  vicinity  of  the  project.  There are two major ethnic groups in the project  area namely The Wampar and The Adzera. The  project  area  includes  13  communities:  Chivasing,  Tararan,  Bampu,  Kokok  and  Nowa  who  belong  to  the  Wampar  ethnic  group,  and  Mangiang,  Siruwarang,  Urai,  WafiBampun,  Waridzian  (Waritsian),  Bagabuang,  Mayamgzariang  and  Gainarun,  who  belong  to  the Adzera ethnic group.

Gender equality.summary: The evidence from the documents FVR_GS6503_MP24102020+to+23102023.public.pdf and GS6503_PNG_MVAR_Monitoring-Report_v7.pdf partially addresses the SDG indicator Gender wage equity. The project acknowledges a pay gap in the technical forest services sector, where men generally earn more, but also mentions that women in non-technical roles tend to earn more than men. However, the evidence does not provide explicit quantitative data that directly measures the ratio of the average wage paid to female employees to male employees in the same position. Therefore, the evidence is qualitative and scores 1.

Clean water and sanitation.summary: The evidence from the document '%281%29Public_Version_Validation+report_040320_PNG_Biomas_MV_AR_project.pdf' on page 120-124 partially satisfies the measurement requirement of the SDG indicator 'Water retention capacity'. The project's implementation of measures to conserve water, such as using rainwater collection systems and minimizing groundwater requirements, is mentioned. However, the evidence does not provide explicit quantitative data that directly measures the water retention capacity. The project's buffer zones and water treatment system are also mentioned, but these are qualitative measures that do not directly measure the indicator variable. Therefore, the score is 1.

Decent work and economic growth.summary: The evidence does not directly measure the indicator variable 'Total number of employees earning above local minimum wage' as the local minimum wage is not explicitly stated in the provided context. The total number of employees in the project is mentioned, but this information does not help to determine the number of employees earning above the local minimum wage. The context provides information on average hourly earnings, but this does not directly relate to the local minimum wage or the number of employees earning above it. Therefore, the evidence fails to satisfy the measurement requirement.

Gender Equality Requirements and Guidelines v1.1  effective from 1st March 2018: 2.0 GENDER SENSITIVE REQUIREMENTS  (STEPS 1-3)  2.1 STEP 1: BASIC CONTEXT  2.1.1  Provide evidence that the Project concept and  design  covers  the  overall  societal  context  from  a  gendered perspective.  2.1.1.1  Align  the  project  to  the  Gold  Standard  Gender Policy.  WHY IS THIS IMPORTANT?  The  main  objective  of  the  Gender  Policy  is  to  strengthen  the  Gold  Standard  and  its  stakeholders’  responsiveness  to  the  multiple,  culturally-derived  principles  of  gender  equality  and  women’s  empowerment  and  to  address  and  account  for  the  links  between  gender  equality,  natural  resources  management and environmental sustainability.  REQUIREMENTS  The Gender Policy applies to all projects applying for  Gold  Standard  certification,  spells  out  the  principles  for  achieving  Gender  Responsive  certification  at  the  performance  level,  and  through  supporting  technical  guidance  on  gender,  solidifies  the  operational  requirements  for  stakeholder  involvement  and  partnership  in  the  design,  implementation,  and  evaluation of projects.  Question 1:  Does  the  project  reflect  the  key  issues  and  requirements  of  Gender  Sensitive  design  and  implementation as outlined in the Gender Policy?  Explain how. | Validation opinion: B By interviewing the stakeholders, the validation team  has  found  that  there  is  a  huge  gender  gap  in  the  country  and  as  a  result  of  land  being  traditionally  owned  by  men.  Generally,  men  have  access  and  control over the resources and benefits derived from  the  land  The  important  decisions  are  being  traditionally taken by men in the family in PNG.  The  validation  team  has  observed  that  women  can  work  in  the  plantations  establishment  and  the  intercropping  system.  The  gender  impacts  of  the  project  have  already  been  identified  and  mitigation  measures  are  being  implemented.  The  project  director  interviewed  informed  that  its  policy  is  to   increase  the  number  of  women  in  the  field  when  including  them  in  the  plantation  establishment  activities, such as planting.  ut  the  project  commissioned  a  social  impact  assessment/5/  where  all  the  gender-related  risks  were identified following the categorization presented  in  the  World  Bank’s  Papua  New  Guinea  -  Country  gender assessment for the period 2011-2012 (CGA).  The validation team has reviewed this and accepted  the same.  As gender inequality was identified in the region, the  stakeholder  consultation  was  also  developed  following  a  gender-sensitive  approach,  which  included  a  separate  meeting  for  women.  The  validation  team  has  reviewed  the  LSC  for  women  and accepted the same.

However, it does not provide explicit quantitative data to measure the economic value generated locally through employment in sustainable tourism activities. The Public Version Validation report (Document: %281%29Public_Version_Validation+report_040320_PNG_Biomas_MV_AR_project.pdf, Page: 119) mentions increased income levels through project employment, plantation-land cropshare, and annual land rentals, but does not provide specific quantitative values. Therefore, the evidence does not directly measure the indicator variable.

Although the project has returned PGK16.3 million in local investment, this is not directly related to ecotourism income. The context mentions land rental payments, carbon payments, and paid work as benefits to the community, but does not provide specific total income figures, percentages of the local population engaged in ecotourism-related employment, average annual income earned by individuals, or the proportion of ecotourism income reinvested in the local community. The context also does not provide information on new job opportunities created in the ecotourism sector.

P.9 Environment, Ecology and Land Use 
(P.9.8) demonstrate effective engagement of 
landowners, 
particularly 
women, 
in 
intercropping activities.
The preparation of land, 
establishment of plantations, and financial 
literacy training for women have empowered 
them to generate income through crop sales.
This validates the principle, showing that the 
project is benefiting both the environment and 
gender equality goals.

cropland.

tropical moist climate region is 79.3% of the planted area.
The 
remaining 20.7% is in the tropical wet climate region.

Decent work and economic growth.summary: The evidence from document FVR_GS6503_MP24102020+to+23102023.public.pdf, page 48, provides quantitative data on average hourly earnings of employees by sex. The data shows a significant disparity in earnings between female and male employees in the technical forest services sector, with average hourly earnings for female employees being 5.92 Kinas in 2020, 0.00 Kinas in 2021, and 8.78 Kinas in 2022, compared to 5.92 Kinas, 29.53 Kinas, and 25.68 Kinas for male employees in the same years. This evidence directly measures the indicator variable and satisfies the measurement requirement.

12. Responsible consumption and producti.summary: The evidence does not directly measure the average reduction in synthetic fertilizer consumption per ha. Although the amount of fertilizer used is reduced from an unspecified baseline to 52 kg/ha, resulting in 0.104 tCO2/ha (Document: GS6503_DC_Validation_public.pdf, Page: 11), this value represents the emission factor, not the baseline level of synthetic fertilizer consumption per ha. The distribution of planted area is 90% grassland and 10% cropland (Document: GS6503_PNG_MVAR_Monitoring-Report_v7.pdf, Page: 31), but this information does not directly measure the indicator variable. Therefore, the evidence is qualitative and does not satisfy the indicator's measurement requirement.

The  Baseline  is  the  estimated  carbon  stock  that  would occur in the baseline scenario.   The  baseline  scenario  describes  the  activities  that  would occur in the absence of the proposed project.  2. The Baseline shall be determined by estimating the  ‘tree’  and  ‘non-tree’  biomass  that  is  present  in  the  eligible planting area just prior to the planting start.  3.  To  determine  the  Baseline  of  the  eligible  planting  area the land shall be:  (a)  stratified  according  to  its  vegetation  types  (grassland, bushland, etc.), AND  (b) for each of these strata scientifically based project- specific1,  regional  or  national  default  values  shall  be  found  which  state  ‘tree’  and  ‘non-tree’  biomass  of  these vegetation types.  International  default  values  from  the  IPCC  shall  only  be used if no other values are available.  4.  The  Baseline  shall  be  determined  on  a  Modelling  Unit (MU) level using the following formula:  Baseline  =  Baseline  Eligible  planting  area  /  Eligible  planting area  The Baseline is deducted in the first year (t=1).  5. The  Baseline  is  the  estimated  carbon  stock  that  would occur in the baseline scenario.   The  baseline  scenario  describes  the  activities  that  would occur in the absence of the proposed project.  2. The Baseline shall be determined by estimating the  ‘tree’  and  ‘non-tree’  biomass  that  is  present  in  the  eligible planting area just prior to the planting start.  3.  To  determine  the  Baseline  of  the  eligible  planting  area the land shall be:  (a)  stratified  according  to  its  vegetation  types  (grassland, bushland, etc.), AND  (b) for each of these strata scientifically based project- specific1,  regional  or  national  default  values  shall  be  found  which  state  ‘tree’  and  ‘non-tree’  biomass  of  these vegetation types.  International  default  values  from  the  IPCC  shall  only  be used if no other values are available.  4.  The  Baseline  shall  be  determined  on  a  Modelling  Unit (MU) level using the following formula:  Baseline  =  Baseline  Eligible  planting  area  /  Eligible  planting area  The Baseline is deducted in the first year (t=1).  5. Responsible consumption and producti.summary: The evidence from the document '%281%29Public_Version_Validation+report_040320_PNG_Biomas_MV_AR_project.pdf' on page 127-128 mentions the project's measures to minimize the use of herbicides and fertilizers, and the use of glyphosate only when necessary. How can the use of environmentally sound.content: Safeguarding principles and Requirements v1.2: 3   3   (   (   (   (   (   (   3   3   3 Principle 9.5 - Hazardous and Non-hazardous  Waste   The Gold Standard Certification requires that   3.9.11 Projects shall avoid or, when avoidance is not  feasible,  minimise  and  control  release  of  hazardous  materials  resulting  from  their  production,  transportation,  handling,  storage  and  use  in  the  Project. Where avoidance is not possible, the health  risks,  including  potential  differentiated  effects  on  men,  women  and  children,  of  the  potential  use  of  hazardous  materials  shall  be  addressed  appropriately.   .9.12  Projects  shall  consider  the  use  of  less  hazardous  substitutes  for  such  chemicals  and  materials and  will avoid the manufacture, trade, and  use of chemicals and hazardous materials subject to  international  bans  or  phase-outs  due  to  their  high  toxicity  to  living  organisms,  environmental  persistence,  potential  for  bioaccumulation,  or  potential for depletion of the ozone layer15.   .9.13 All sources of waste and waste products shall  be  identified  and  classified.  Waste  products  include  amongst others:   a) Chemical wastes, AND   b) Containers, AND   c) Fuels and oils, AND   d) Human waste, AND   e) Rubbish (including metals, plastics, organic and  paper products), AND   f) Abandoned buildings, machinery or equipment.   .9.14 Where waste generation may not be avoided,  the Project shall reduce the generation of waste, and  recover and reuse waste in a manner that is safe for  human health and the environment.   .9.15  Where  waste  may  not  be  recovered  or  reused, it shall be treated, destroyed, or disposed of  in  an  environmentally  sound  manner  that  includes  the  appropriate  control  of  emissions  and  residues  resulting  from  the  handling  and  processing  of  the  waste material.   .9.16  If  the  generated  waste  is  considered  hazardous16,  reasonable  alternatives  for  its | Validation opinion: The  validation  team  has  reviewed  the  environment  impact  assessment  report  and  PP  discussed  about  key  management  measures  for  each  of  these  are  presented below.  Direct Application of Chemicals, Ash and Biosolids to  Land:  The use and application of chemicals to land will be  minimised  as  far  as  practicable.  The  use  of  herbicides  and  fertiliser  will  be  minimised  and  the  use of pesticide  is not  proposed  unless a significant  pest  problem  is  encountered.  Additional  management measures have been incorporated into  the waste management procedure include:  1.Only  chemicals  with  proven  environmental  safety  and  not  subject  to  bans  or  phase-outs  will  be  used;  chemicals  used  in  plantations  will  be  approved  for  use  under  FSC  guidelines  Spraying  of  glyphosate  (considered  to  be  a  non-residual  herbicide)  will  be  minimised,  with  no  spraying  to  occur  within  the  established riparian buffer zones.  2.Ash to be reused in road construction (bottom ash)  or as fertiliser for the plantations (fly ash) will be  subjected to the laboratory leaching tests for  confirmation of the anticipated low risk related to its  reuse.  3.Biosolids will only be used as a fertiliser in areas of  the plantation that are not used to grow food crops.  Hydrocarbon or Chemical Leakage, Spillage and  Disposal:  Proper  design,  quality  control  and  operating  procedures  will  minimise  the  risk  associated  with  spills or leaks including the proper storage,  use and  disposal of these potentially hazardous materials and  routinely  maintaining  and  inspecting  equipment.  Additional  management  measures  have  been  incorporated  into  the  waste  management  procedure  which  address  fuels  and  chemicals,  and  include  measures such as:  1.All  fuels  and  chemicals  will  be  securely  stored  in  bunded areas within the bund design limits, and fuels  and oils will be stored in appropriate and maintained  receptacles  in  an  undercover  area,  away  from  waterbodies and office/living quarters.  2.A  Spill  Response  Plan  will  be  developed  and  implemented  and  the  required  equipment  maintained.  3.Inductions  and  training  will  be  provided  to  staff  concerning  handling  hazardous  materials  and  implementing the Spill Response Plan.  4.Refuelling and servicing of vehicles and equipment  will  be  undertaken  in  a  designated  bunded  area  close to the fuel storage area.  5.Liquid  waste  produced  in  the  workshop  will  be

The  mitigation measures include   1.Mix,  store,  secure  and  dispose  glyphosate  so  that  leaks and spillages are avoided.  2.Limit  spraying  next  to  riparian  buffers  in  drier  months, where feasible.  3.Limit  harvesting  to  the  drier  months  in  areas  next  to riparian buffers, where feasible.  4.Implement  an  adaptive  management  plan  that  seeks to maximise efficiencies between weed control  and volume application of glyphosate.  5.Monitor riparian buffer zones, particularly along the  borders of plantations  6. Avoid spraying during high winds.

It also relates to equal pay  for  work  of  equal  value.  The  validation  team  has  checked  the  calculation  of  ex-ante  estimation  of  outcomes and accepted the same.   DG 13:  SDG  13  was  the  only  SDG  considered  for  monetization  and  consequent  ex-ante  estimation.  Thus,  CO2  emission  reduction  was  estimated  for  the  forest  plantations  according  to  two  different  regimes that will take place in the project.   The first regime is the “Biomass regime” and will be  implemented  in  most  of  the  project  area  (+12.000  ha). This regime consists in the production of wood  for  the  unique  purpose  of  running  the  power  plant.  This regime has a rotation age of 7 years and does  not  have  thinning.  The  other  regime  is  known  as  “Veneer  regime”  and  consist  in  the  production  of  wood for veneer production. The rotation age of this  regime is 15 years and the management includes 3  thinings  at  4,  9  and  12  years  for  removing    40%,  40%  and  50%  of  the  biomass,  respectively.  Wood  that  is  thinned  will  go  to  the  power  plant.  The  validation  team  has  checked  the  calculation  of  ex- ante  estimation  of  outcomes  and  accepted  the  same.   DG 15:  The  baseline  scenario  is  composed  of  house  gardens  and  degraded  pastures.  Thus,  the  Proportion  of  Forest  Area  in  the  baseline  would  remain as zero. The net benefit related to this SDG  is  calculated  as  the  PFA  in  the  project  scenario  minus the PFA in the baseline. The validation team  has checked the calculation of ex-ante estimation of  outcomes and accepted the same.

Such  measurements  are  required  if  evidence  for  applicability  (as  listed  above)  of  literature  values  is  deemed insufficient by an auditor.   c)  The  project  owner  shall  use  precision  of  20%  of  the  mean  at  the  90%  confidence  level  as  the  criteria  for  reliability of estimates   .1.8 Special Guidance for Smallholder Projects   a)  Data  is  derived  from  peer  reviewed  published  literature.   Evidence  for  applicability  of  the  literature  values  to  the  project  site  has  to  be  provided  with  respect  to  climate  factors  (e.g.  precipitation  levels  and  seasonal  distribution),  soil  and  vegetation  types  as  well  as  current  and  historic  management  systems  (e.g.  crops,  tillage  techniques, fertilization).   Direct  application  of  literature  values  is  only  permitted  if  the  source  conditions  match  the  project  environment,  evidence of which shall be provided.   Furthermore, literature values shall only be applied within  the  spatial  and  temporal  dimensions  analysed  in  the  original  source  (e.g.  SOC  depth,  timespan  for  which  changes are documented).   If  a  range  of  parameter  values  are  given  in  a  source  or  data  is  aggregated  across  various  factor  levels  (e.g.  average  in  a  region,  across  a  range  of  soil  types),  the  most conservative value shall be used.   b)  Alternatively,  values  from  literature  may  be  verified  by  comparing  them  to  measurements  in  a  set  of  sample  sites  within  the  respective  project  stratum  to  indicate  conservativeness  of  the  parameter  values  applied.   Such  measurements  are  required  if  evidence  for  applicability  (as  listed  above)  of  literature  values  is  deemed insufficient by an auditor.   c) If the uncertainty of estimates is less than or equal  to  20%  of  the  mean  change  value  then  the  project  owner  may  use  the  estimated  value  without  any  deduction  for  uncertainty, i.e. UD = 0. If the uncertainty is greater than  20% of the mean value, then the project owner shall use  the  estimated  value  subject  to  an  Uncertainty  Deduction  (UD) in table 1 below (see approach 3). | Validation opinion:

Recommended  Best  Practice  for  Continuous  Input  &  Grievance  Expression  from  Stakeholders  (local  governance meetings)   The  Gold  Standard  recommends  that,  where  practical,  project  developers  have  regular  meetings  to  invite  local  stakeholders to give their feedback on the project, ensure  that  the  project  goals  are  understood  and  investigate  if  there are any improvements that could be made.   hese could be in the form of:   Annual  project  open  days  to  allow  local  stakeholders  to  visit the site and see the project   A  meeting  (e.g.  coincide  with  training  and  repairs,  or  at  the same time as auditor field visits) that includes general  information  about  the  project,  education  about  climate  change and carbon offsetting, etc.   These regular meetings can be very useful for projects as  they  allow  project  developers  to  hear  the  views  of  local  stakeholders (including employees) and, as they allow for  greater  communication  and  understanding,  can  improve  relations between the project and the local community.   If regular meetings are planned, they shall be mentioned  at  both  rounds  of  the  LSC  and  advertised  in  accordance  with   he Gold Standard requirements.   As part of the Validation, the VVB shall check whether the  approved/selected  methods  of  continuous  input  &  grievance from the SC Report or other consultations have  been  implemented  on  site  and  discussed  in  the  project  documentation.   The continuous input & grievance log (the template table  in  Section  2.0)  is  part  of  the  project  documentation  that  the auditor shall use to audit the project.   he GSVVB shall check:  a)  That  the  project  developer  has  responded  in  a  reasonable manner to comments that have been raised.   (b)  That  the  responses  are  adequate,  timely  and  appropriate to address the problem or comments raised.   (c)  That  any  issues  the  auditor  considers  serious  are  taken  up  as  a  Forward  Action  Request  (FAR)  for  the  project  as  part  of  the  certification  to  ensure  further  monitoring of the issue.   The  auditor  shall  make  use  of  the  comments  when  in  discussions  with  local  stakeholders  as  part  of  the  field  visits.   The  comments  can  provide  useful  starting  points  for  conversations with the local stakeholders.   If there are comments from a stakeholder that has chosen  not  to  remain  anonymous,  the  auditor  can  request  to  speak to this individual if they think an issue is of serious  concern for the project. The total biomass stock in forests, including both above and below ground components, within a specific geographic scope for MU-002 is estimated to be 25.7 million tons in 2018, but the data is not available for the entire time period. Therefore, the score is 2.

15. Life on land.summary: The evidence provided in the Intermediate Answers and Context does not directly measure the total area of trees planted. However, the cumulative planted area is provided for each year, which is 302 ha in 2016, 856 ha in 2017, 1,976 ha in 2018, 2,562 ha in 2019, 2,895 ha in 2020, 3,220 ha in 2021, 3,412 ha in 2022, and 3,759 ha in 2023. This quantitative data directly measures the indicator variable, but the total cumulative planted area over the 8-year period is not explicitly stated. Therefore, the evidence partially satisfies the measurement requirement.

The underperformance is attributable to several factors:    1.  Plantation Failure: Poor soil conditions have led to unsuccessful plantation  efforts.  2.  Drought in 2021: Severe drought conditions in 2021 adversely affected the  project outcomes.  3.  COVID-19 Impact: The pandemic hindered access to land and prevented  the acquisition of necessary permits. Additionally, the restrictions imposed  during the pandemic disrupted the QA/QC process for the planting areas.  4.  Inability to access and obtain land.  he ex-ante CO2 fixation values were calculated using the published Mean Annual  Increment (MAI) and wood density data for Eucalyptus Pelltia. These calculations  incorporated IPCC values for Biomass Expansion Factors(BEF) and the root-to- shoot ratio to estimate biomass. Standard conversion factors for carbon and CO2  were then applied to convert biomass into CO2. Emissions from harvesting were  included in these estimations, as the project was initially  designed for rotational  harvesting.  umulative  CO2  fixation  is  determined  annually  by  summing  the  CO2  fixed  or  released  by  the  project  across  all  the  modelling  units(MUs).  Long-term  CO2  fixation  is  calculated  as  the  average  cumulative  CO2  fixation  over  the  project’s  lifespan,  following  the  guidelines  of  the  Gold  Standard  for  Agriculture  (GS-AR)  methodology. This value represents the maximum number of CO2 certificates the  project can generate throughout the entire crediting period. | :  | :  | :  | :

15. Life on land.summary: The evidence provided directly measures the indicator variable 'Total protected Land Area' with explicit quantitative data. The total land area of the project that has been designated as protected is 501.12 hectares, which is confirmed by the verifier opinion in the validation report (Document: FVR_GS6503_MP24102020+to+23102023.public.pdf, Page: 43). The protected area is a minimum of 10% of the total project area, which is 24,913 ha (Document: GS6503_DC_Validation_public.pdf, Page: 12). The percentage of the project area that is protected land area is 9.8% (2,427 / 24,913 * 100), which is confirmed by the verifier opinion in the validation report (Document: FVR_GS6503_MP24102020+to+23102023.public.pdf, Page: 43). The evidence satisfies the indicator's measurement requirement.

Requirements of Combined tool to identify the  baseline  scenario  and  demonstrate  additionality  in  A/R  CDM  project  activities,  Version 01: Step 4: Common practice  Analysis of similar activities to the  proposed project activity | Validation opinion: The validation team has accepted that the establishment of forest  plantations is not a common practice in PNG where the project is  being  developed,  considering  the  barriers  that  exist  for  the  development  of  this  activity  at  the  local  level.  Although  the  development  of  commercial  forest  plantations  could  become  a  priority  economic  activity  in  the  country,  given  the  limitations  of  traditional  agriculture,  different  barriers  hinder  its  implementation  in the project area, being the most significant barriers: land tenure  problems,  lack  of  good  transport  infrastructure  and  skilled  labor.  Hence  the  proposed  project  activity  is  not  a  common  practice  in  PNG.  The  validation  team  reviewed  the  historic  annual  reforestation areas for PNG and convinced that the project is not  common practice in PNG. Hence the project is additional.

Life on land.summary: The evidence provided in the Intermediate Answers and Context does not directly measure the indicator variable 'Trends in species diversity' as described in the SDG indicator 15.8. The number of tree species present in the forest ecosystem is 370, but this does not directly measure species diversity. The proportion of native species is 63.5%, but this is also not a direct measure of species diversity. The eligible planted area is 2,248 ha, but this is related to forest area, not species diversity. The verification team confirms that the TA forest percentage is correctly calculated as 9.0%, but this is also not a direct measure of species diversity. Therefore, the score is 0.

Life on land.summary: The evidence from the Project+Annual+Report+Markham+Valley+AR+2023+Report+-+signed.pdf, Page: 13, mentions that the project aims to protect or enhance biological diversity by identifying and managing existing patches of native tree species. However, this statement does not directly measure the indicator variable 'Trends in species distribution'. The evidence from the same document, Page: 15, mentions that the project's invasive alien species management procedure ensures that any risks to the soil, ecology, and water are monitored and reported, but this statement is also qualitative and does not directly measure the indicator variable. Therefore, the evidence does not satisfy the indicator's measurement requirement.

In  the  case  of  downstream  impacts,  the  Project  shall  ensure  mitigation  is  in  place  within  the  Project  Boundary  such  that  the  Project shall not adversely affect these areas:   a) Existing patches of native tree species, AND   b) Single solitary stems of native tree species, AND   c)  All  freshwater  resources  including  rivers,  lakes,  swamps, ephemeral water bodies and wells   d)  Habitats  of  rare,  threatened  and  endangered  species, AND   e)  Areas  relevant  for  habitat  connectivity  shall  be  identified  and  managed  to  protect  or  enhance  biological diversity.   .9.34  If  the  Project  is  located  in  such  habitats;  the  Project Developer shall:   a) Minimise unwarranted conversion or degradation  of the habitat.   b)  Identify  opportunities  to  enhance  the  habitat  as  part  of  the  Project. The  establishment of plantation also reduces soil erosion.  The  slope  topography  of  the  project  area  ensured  that there is not soil compaction due to movement of  heavy machinery and no soil pressure. The different  soil  types  have  been  mapped  out  and  prevention  of  soil erosion. The biota planted also acts as a barrier  against  soil  erosion.  Though  soil  erosion  is  not  completely  prevented,  some  restitution  efforts  have  been done for restoration. In general, the established  plantation follows the land contours by designing the  rip lines accordingly.   Key  management  measures  to  minimise  impacts  to  soils  from  erosion,  compaction  and/or  mixing  and  nutrient loss are described below.  Vegetation Clearing and Construction  1. Vegetation clearing will only be undertaken to the  extent  required  for  the  designated  land  use;  boundaries  of  areas  to  be  cleared  will  be  physically  demarcated prior to commencement of clearing.  2. At the completion of ground disturbance activities,  where  feasible,  all  disturbed  areas  will  be  rehabilitated  to  minimise  ongoing  soil  erosion  and  promote the natural revegetation of these areas.  3.  Where  practicable,  road  construction  and  major  earthworks will occur during the dry season.  4.  Where  possible,  areas  to  be  cleared  will  exclude  locations  of  high  erosion  potential  (e.g.,  steep  slopes).  5.Project facilities will be located in already disturbed  areas as far as practicable.  6.All  removed  topsoil  and  vegetation  will  be  stockpiled  for  later  use  during  rehabilitation  or  recycled.  7.Erosion  and  sediment  control  measures  together  with  locations  and  types  of  control  devices  (e.g.,  sediment  pond,  silt  fence,  diversion  drain)  will  be  installed, where required.  8.Groundcover vegetation will be retained if possible.  9.The length of time that cleared/disturbed areas are  exposed  will  be  minimised  to  the  greatest  extent  practicable.  10.Topsoil  will  be  carefully  removed  and  stockpiled  when an area is being cleared for subsequent use in  rehabilitation where feasible.  11.Revegetation of stockpiles will be promoted to  protect soil from erosion, discourage weeds  and maintain soil microbe populations.  12.Vegetation  clearing  will  adhere  to  the  PNG  Logging Code of Practice (PNGFA/DEC, 1996).  he  validation  team  has  reviewed  the  environment  management  plan  prepared  by  ERIAS  group  in  March  2017  and  accepted  that  mitigation  measures  to safeguard the environment aspects as robust. The  mitigation measures include   1.Mix,  store,  secure  and  dispose  glyphosate  so  that  leaks and spillages are avoided.  2.Limit  spraying  next  to  riparian  buffers  in  drier  months, where feasible.  3.Limit  harvesting  to  the  drier  months  in  areas  next  to riparian buffers, where feasible.  4.Implement  an  adaptive  management  plan  that  seeks to maximise efficiencies between weed control  and volume application of glyphosate.  5.Monitor riparian buffer zones, particularly along the  borders of plantations  6.
